intro: Efficient API management requires understanding rate limits, throughput, and load calculations. Converting between requests per minute (RPM), requests per second (RPS), requests per hour (RPH), and requests per day (RPD) helps prevent rate limit errors and service degradation. Free and fast conversions. All values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you up to date.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **RPM ÷ 60 = RPS**. Direct calculation with your input values. Convert between RPM, RPS, RPH, and RPD instantly.

explanation:
API rate limiting is essential for protecting backend services, managing traffic, and preventing abuse. Rate limits are commonly expressed in requests per minute (RPM), requests per second (RPS), requests per hour (RPH), or requests per day (RPD).

## How to Calculate

This calculator converts between different rate limit units and helps you understand API throughput. Enter a value and its unit to instantly see equivalent rates across all common units.

## Final Notes

This calculator is for reference only. For critical decisions (SLA agreements, production infrastructure), consult with a systems architect or cloud engineer. Values are current as of 2026.

faq.q: What is a burst allowance in rate limiting?
faq.a: A burst allowance lets APIs accept short spikes above the standard rate limit, useful for handling sudden traffic.

faq.q: What should I do when I hit a 429 status code?
faq.a: Implement exponential backoff: wait 1 second, then 2 seconds, then 4 seconds, etc., before retrying your request.

faq.q: What's the difference between per-user and global rate limits?
faq.a: Per-user limits apply to individual API keys or users; global limits apply to your entire application's total requests.

faq.q: How can I reduce the number of API calls?
faq.a: Use response caching to avoid repeated requests to the same endpoint with unchanged data.

faq.q: Can I batch multiple API requests together?
faq.a: Yes, group multiple operations into single requests when the API supports batch operations to stay under rate limits.
